questions.prompt: Why does money work better than direct bartering?
options: It's heavier and harder to lose
It lets you trade with anyone, not just someone who happens to want exactly what you have
It's required by law in every country
questions.explanation: Money is a flexible tool for trading value — you don't need to find someone who wants your exact item to get what you need.

questions.prompt: A limited sneaker release sells out in minutes and resells for triple the price online. What's driving that price?
options: The shoes got more expensive to manufacture
questions.explanation: When lots of people want something and there isn't much of it, price rises — classic supply and demand.

questions.prompt: What does inflation mean for money that just sits still in cash for years?
options: It quietly buys less over time
questions.explanation: Prices tend to rise over time, so unchanging cash buys less and less as years pass.

questions.prompt: You already own a working laptop. A newer model just released. Buying it would be a...
options: Neither — it doesn't count as spending
questions.explanation: You already have a working version, so the newer model is an upgrade — a want, not a need.

questions.prompt: You spend $80 on concert tickets instead of putting it toward a $600 laptop goal. What's the real cost of that decision?
options: The $80 plus being $80 further from your laptop goal
There's no cost since it's your own money
questions.explanation: That's opportunity cost — every dollar spent one way is a dollar that can't go toward something else.

questions.prompt: A website flashes "Only 2 left! Offer ends in 5 minutes!" What's this tactic designed to do?
options: Create urgency so you skip comparing prices or thinking it over
questions.explanation: Urgency tactics are built to short-circuit careful decision-making, whether or not the scarcity claim is even true.

questions.prompt: Using the 50/30/20 rule, how much of a $400 paycheck should go toward wants?
questions.explanation: 30% of $400 is $120 — the wants slice of the split.

questions.prompt: Your monthly income is $500 and your needs cost $220. How much is left for wants and savings combined?
questions.explanation: $500 − $220 = $280 left over once needs are covered.

questions.prompt: You budgeted $60 for wants this month but you're already at $75 with a week left. What's the smartest move?
options: Quit budgeting since it isn't working
Notice it now and cut back for the rest of the month, then adjust next month's plan
questions.explanation: Catching an overspend mid-month lets you course-correct instead of discovering it too late to fix.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to "pay yourself first"?
options: Save whatever happens to be left at the end of the month
Move money to savings the moment you're paid, before spending anything else
Only save money on your birthday
questions.explanation: Paying yourself first treats savings like a required bill, taken out before any spending happens.

questions.prompt: Which savings goal is written the SMART way — specific and measurable?
questions.explanation: A specific amount and deadline turn a vague wish into an actual plan you can track.

questions.prompt: Which of these is what an emergency fund is actually for?
options: A concert ticket sale you don't want to miss
questions.explanation: Emergency funds cover unplanned, necessary costs — not wants, even exciting or time-sensitive ones.

questions.prompt: Where should money you're actively spending week-to-week live?
questions.explanation: Checking accounts are built for frequent transactions like debit card purchases and transfers.

questions.prompt: What happens when you spend more than what's in your checking account and overdraft coverage is turned on?
options: The purchase is simply declined, no cost
The purchase usually goes through, but you get charged a fee, often around $35
Your bank account is automatically closed
questions.explanation: Overdraft coverage lets purchases go through even without enough funds — for a fee that's often much bigger than the purchase itself.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between a debit card and a credit card?
options: A debit card spends your own money now; a credit card is borrowed money you pay back later
Debit cards can only be used online
questions.explanation: Debit pulls directly from what you already have. Credit is a loan you're expected to repay — a very different risk profile.

questions.prompt: Do you need to be 18 to open any bank account?
options: No — teen or custodial accounts exist, usually with a parent as co-owner
questions.explanation: Many banks offer accounts for minors, typically supervised or co-owned by a parent until 18.

questions.prompt: You worked 12 hours at $15/hour. What's your gross pay?
questions.explanation: 12 × $15 = $180 gross — the full amount before any taxes come out.

questions.prompt: Your gross pay is $180 and $25 gets withheld for taxes. What's your net pay, and which number should you budget from?
options: It doesn't matter which one you use
questions.explanation: $180 − $25 = $155. Net pay is what actually lands in your account, so it's the number your budget should be built around.

questions.prompt: A side hustle earns $40 profit but takes 5 hours including prep and cleanup. What's the effective hourly rate?
questions.explanation: $40 ÷ 5 hours = $8/hour — always divide by total time spent, not just the fun parts.

questions.prompt: State minimum wage is higher than federal minimum wage where you live. Which one legally applies to you?
options: Whichever is higher — state, in this case
questions.explanation: Employers must pay at least the highest minimum wage that applies — federal, state, or city, whichever is greatest.
